summary: In this Posit Data Science Hangout, Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins, architecture, and growing adoption. He explains how DuckDB emerged from frustrations teaching Spark courses and conversations with the R community about real data problems. He emphasizes that DuckDB's in-process architecture eliminates client-server complexity, runs anywhere from laptops to browsers, and handles mid-range data that previously required big data solutions. He addresses corporate adoption, noting DuckDB is widely embedded in BI tools and data platforms, and discusses the project's culture of friendliness and controlled governance.
key_points: DuckDB originated from seeing students and experienced practitioners fail to use Spark, revealing that common data tasks were unnecessarily difficult
The in-process architecture means no server, no passwords, no ports — it runs inside your R or Python process and can directly read/write data frames
Corporate adoption is massive but often invisible, with DuckDB embedded inside BI tools, data transformation platforms, and even cars
DuckDB intentionally avoids open governance to prevent the 'unmaintainable mess' fate of many Apache projects that tried to please everyone
The team was initially ridiculed for not building a distributed system, but held firm on the belief that single-node processing handles far more than people assumed
money_quotes: I find it always funny when people sit in front of a $3,000 sort of Mac book that runs a web browser and nothing else. And then they use that to spend more money on Snowflake in order to query like a bunch of rows.

We were initially ridiculed for not having a distributed system. And it was our strong belief, and hill we were willing to die on, that you don't actually need distributed systems in order to query significant amounts of data.
After a couple of years of giving this course, we started thinking like, yeah, maybe there's something there. Maybe that doesn't have to be so difficult to write a parquet file.

There is no sort of democracy. That sounds a bit terrible, I have to admit. There's no democracy in terms of direction of the project. And that's intentional because we've seen specifically Apache projects to go down the drain because they couldn't agree on a vision.
It doesn't have a password because there's no point of having a password. It's right there in your thing. It's yours.
Maybe we want to treat high value data better than sticking it in a CSV file. Maybe we do want to have a schema. Maybe we do want to make sure that we have the right types.
If you look at what will have the biggest sort of impact for the most people, that's something that I think is worthwhile.
I was ready to sort of be ridiculed for much longer than we actually were.
key_insight: DuckDB's success came from observing that most data practitioners were struggling with unnecessarily complex tools, and that a simple in-process database could replace distributed systems for the vast majority of real-world analytical workloads.
